text:
@Pennaz91 That's okay. I'm so old I actually remember this ad, although I was preschooler (I was born in '59). For years I thought I'd made it up or something, based on Andy Griffith and Buddy Ebsen doing similar ads at the end of their shows. Then, along came the internet, and I discovered it wasn't a false memory.

I'm old enough that I went "WTF?" when they started making "The Flintstones" more juvenile. I wasn't old enough to put my finger on it, but I could see the difference in tone.

text: @racookster I totally respect your opinion, but I still don't feel confortable with this commercial! That doesn't change the fact that the Flintstones show did... Rocks! For shure!
author: @Pennaz91

text:
@racookster I knew that the Flintstones were made, at first, for an adult audience, but the mix of "cigarettes + Fred" doesn't work either! 
The Flintstones is a show, for the most, enjoyable for all the ages. 
It is cartoony, sometimes pretty dumb in gags and stories, but it never gets too much absurd or childish. 
Watching them smoking and saying how much doing it is good... Doesn't fit for me! 

As much as it would be watching Homer Simpson making a TRUE commercial about the beer!
author: @Pennaz91

text:
@Pennaz91 As others have pointed out, "The Flintstones" didn't start out as a kids' show. It was on at prime-time. It was sort of "The Simpsons" of its day. Now, watch as some mope comes along and says, "I can't believe they're selling cigarettes to kids!"

They weren't. As I said in my last post, not only were adults assumed to be competent to make their own risky decisions in the 1960s, they were also expected to monitor what their kids saw on TV and... you know, be parents.
author: @racookster

text:
This is so much distant from what the Flintstones were! I don't know exactly why... But mixing up Hanna & Barbera with cigarettes doesn't work! 

What about a commercial with Tom'n Jerry drinking vodka then?
author: @Pennaz91

text: Actually, it's a misconception that cigarettes were thought to be harmless when this was made. In the 1940 (expanded in '53) novella "If This Goes On--" by Robert A. Heinlein, one character says to another, "It's a filthy, disgusting habit. They'll probably give me cancer, but I happen to like filthy, disgusting habits." The hazards were known. What has changed is that adults are no longer assumed to be competent to make their own risky lifestyle decisions. Back then, they were.
